papers.title: Evaluation of the density of steels
papers.paper_scope: Development of regression models for the prediction of steel density from chemical composition, using thermodynamic analysis to account for cementite in ferritic steels and TiC in austenitic steels.

alloys.alloys_notes: Studied composition ranges (max weight percent from 44 batches): C 0.49%, Cr 2.58%, Cu 0.69%, Mn 1.88%, Mo 1.1%, Ni 1.18%, S 0.05%, Si 1.98%, V 0.36%, Al ≤0.03%, Nb ≤0.04%, P ≤0.04%, Ti ≤0.02%. Other elements present in minor amounts; see Table 1.

Studied composition ranges from 64 batches (weight percent): C 0.003–0.128%, Co 0–4.45%, Cr 14.6–18.97%, Cu 0–4.47%, Mn 0.01–3.89%, Mo 0.01–3.03%, N 0–0.13%, Ni 9.96–15.73%, Si 0.01–1.96%, Ti 0–0.62%, TiC 0–0.51%, other elements at low levels; see Table 4.

processes.description: Literature data: commercial ferritic and low-alloy steels with varying compositions; no detailed processing history available. Thermodynamic equilibrium assumed for cementite formation during heat treatment (temperature set to heat treatment temperature or temperature at which austenite fully transformed).
Literature data: Ti-stabilized austenitic steels; single-phase austenite or austenite + TiC. Thermodynamic analysis performed to determine TiC content and solubilities in austenite.

characterization_methods.purpose: Determine regression coefficients for density of ferritic steels as function of composition, separating cementite effect
characterization_methods.key_findings: Model I (x=steel): R²=0.987, Cu and Mo increase density, C, Cr, Mn, S, Si, V decrease it. Model II (x=ferrite): R²=0.994, with separate cementite density calculation.

characterization_methods.technique: Regression analysis (Equation 4) with thermodynamic analysis of TiC
characterization_methods.purpose: Determine regression coefficients for density of austenitic steels
characterization_methods.key_findings: R²=0.975, TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti reduce density; Co, Cu, Mo, Ni increase density.

characterization_methods.purpose: Compute cementite amount and element solubilities in ferrite/cementite for Model II; also TiC amount in austenitic steels.
characterization_methods.key_findings: Non-linear effect of Cr and Mn on cementite density captured by Equations (6) and (7).

computational_details.simulation_method: Regression analysis using Equation (4) combined with thermodynamic modeling for cementite phase
computational_details.software_used: Thermo-Calc (for thermodynamic calculations of cementite and solubilities)
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Comparison with literature values shown in Table 3 and Figure 1; apparent densities agree with literature for most elements.

analytical_model.model_name: Ferritic steel density Model I (zero solubility in cementite, x=steel)
analytical_model.equation_role: Equation (4) with x=steel; assumes all C bound as cementite and other elements insoluble in cementite
analytical_model.model_note: R²=0.987, maximum deviation 5.9 kg/m³, average deviation 2.0 kg/m³. Regression coefficients from Table 2.

analytical_model.model_name: Ferritic steel density Model II (thermodynamic cementite analysis, x=ferrite)
analytical_model.equation_role: Equation (4) with x=ferrite; cementite amount and solubilities from Thermo-Calc; density of cementite from Equations (6) and (7)
analytical_model.model_note: R²=0.994, maximum deviation 4.2 kg/m³, average deviation 1.4 kg/m³. Regression coefficients from Table 3.

analytical_model.equation_role: Equations (6) and (7) to calculate cementite density as function of Cr and Mn concentration in cementite
analytical_model.parameters: For Cr: ρ_cem = 7742 - 687 * C_Cr^wt (valid when C_Cr>0). For Mn: ρ_cem = 7770 - 741 * C_Mn^wt (valid when C_Mn>0.12); otherwise ρ_cem = 7680 kg/m³.
analytical_model.model_note: Derived from interpolation of literature data [25]; see Figure 2.

computational_details.simulation_method: Regression analysis using Equation (4) with x=austenite, including TiC mass content as a variable; thermodynamic analysis for TiC amount
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Comparison with literature data in Table 4 and Figure 3; apparent densities match literature for most elements except Si.

analytical_model.equation_role: Equation (4) with x=austenite; TiC content included as variable, solubilities in TiC neglected
analytical_model.model_note: R²=0.975, maximum deviation 10.4 kg/m³, average deviation 2.6 kg/m³. Regression coefficients from Table 4.

unmapped_findings: In ferritic steels, Cu and Mo increase density, while C (as cementite), Cr, Mn, S, Si, and V decrease density.
In austenitic steels, TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, and Ti reduce density; Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni increase density.
For substitutional solutes where no regression coefficient is available, theoretical coefficients (1/ρ_i) can give reasonable density estimates.
Significant deviations from ideal behavior are observed for Si and Mn in ferritic steels (covalent bonds in Si, anomalous Mn effect on Curie temperature), and for Si in austenitic steels.
The regression formula 1/ρ_x = Σ r_i C_i^wt(x) provided better correlation than the linear formulas used in earlier studies.
Maximum experimental error reported by Richter is ±0.01% for density measurements.
The paper provides a collection of literature data on density of steels and reviews earlier studies on the effect of solutes on lattice parameter and density of bcc and fcc iron.
